_meta.issue: #171 Rebuild ścieżek — P1 AI w domu (formatywna)
_meta.interaction: Klasyfikacja: w porządku vs ryzykowne (kind: classify)
_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE przykłady SYNTETYCZNE (NotkaApp, przyklad.test, Jan Syntetyczny, Anna Testowa). ZERO realnych danych/PII. Moduł formatywny — bez progu zaliczenia i certyfikatu.

intro: An invitation to Grandma’s birthday, a song for an anniversary, a vacation video with proper editing — image, music, and video generators now do in minutes what used to require a designer and a studio. This module teaches you to use them with joy and with good judgment: what is appropriate, what to label, and whose rights not to violate.

screens.title: What you can generate at home today

items: Images: greeting cards, invitations, illustrations for a family chronicle, an avatar for a messaging app.
Music: a birthday song with the birthday person’s name, a soundtrack for a vacation video.
Video: a slideshow with animations, short clips from a description, automatic captions for recordings.
Retouching: removing strangers from the background, bringing old, damaged family photos back to life.

blocks.text: The secret to a good result is the same as with text prompts: say specifically what you want (theme, style, colors, mood), look at the result, and ask for changes. The first version is a sketch, not a verdict.

blocks.title: Try it today
blocks.text: What is your next family occasion? Generate three versions of a card in different styles (for example, watercolor, retro poster, photo) and pick the best one with someone close to you.

Within the family, nobody demands a certificate of origin on a birthday card. But when content goes wider — to the neighborhood group, the internet, the school — an honest “generated by AI” label builds trust and cuts off misunderstandings. More and more platforms explicitly require it.

items: Label when the content could be mistaken for a real photo or a recording of a real event.
Always label when you publish beyond your private circle — a public profile, a contest, an announcement.
You do not have to label obvious fantasy in a private circle — a dragon over the apartment block speaks for itself.

blocks.title: A hard line
blocks.text: Generating “real” scenes involving actual people — a neighbor, a politician, a classmate — and presenting them as fact is no longer a joke; it is disinformation or a violation of personal rights.

screens.title: Rights to created works in a nutshell

rows: Can I use a generated card privately?
Yes — home use is the safest scenario.
Check the tool’s license: some allow commercial use, some do not.
It depends on the tool and the law — do not automatically assume full copyright.
Can I ask for the style of a specific living artist?
Avoid it — imitating a recognizable style for publication is legally and ethically contested territory.
blocks.textAlt: Table of rights questions: home use is safe, commercial use requires checking the tool’s license, full rights to the result are uncertain, imitating the style of living artists is risky.

blocks.title: One rule instead of a statute
blocks.text: The farther from your own fridge door your creation is headed, the more carefully you read the tool’s license.

screens.title: Likeness: someone else’s face is not raw material

blocks.text: The most common household friction with generators is not about copyright but about people: putting someone’s face into funny scenes without asking, “de-aging” friends’ photos, generating grandchildren who do not exist. Technically simple; between people — it varies.

items: Another person’s face = their consent. Also in jokes, also within the family.
Treat children’s photos with double caution — they cannot give informed consent, and the internet does not forget.
Do not upload other people’s photos to AI tools without their knowledge — it is their likeness traveling to someone else’s server.

blocks.title: A simple test
blocks.text: Would you show that person the result face to face? If you hesitate — do not generate it, or ask first.

interaction.title: Fine or risky?
interaction.intro: Assess these household ideas for creating with AI. The feedback explains where the line runs.

categories.label: Fine
categories.desc: Private or labeled use, without anyone else’s likeness or misleading anyone.

categories.label: Risky / do not do this
categories.desc: Someone else’s likeness without consent, misleading people, violating a license.

items.text: A birthday card for Grandma with a generated bouquet in watercolor style.
items.rationale: Private, joyful use with no one’s likeness — the model scenario.

items.text: Putting a neighbor’s face into memes and sending them to the neighborhood group “for laughs”.
items.rationale: Someone else’s likeness without consent plus a public audience = a violation of personal rights, not a joke.

items.text: A song with Dad’s name generated for his fiftieth birthday, played at the party.
items.rationale: A private occasion, no publication and no one else’s rights — pure joy.

items.text: Selling generated posters without checking whether the tool’s license allows commercial use.
items.rationale: Commerce requires a license — a “free generator” does not mean “free to sell”.

items.text: Restoring a damaged photo of your grandparents from the sixties for the family album.
items.rationale: Retouching a family keepsake for a private album — exactly what these tools are for.

items.text: Generating a realistic “photo” of a local politician in a situation that never happened and posting it on a forum without a label.
items.rationale: A realistic scene + a real person + no label = disinformation with legal consequences.

items.text: A poster for the school fair with AI artwork and the note “illustration generated by AI”.
items.rationale: Public use plus an honest label = a model of transparency.

items.text: Uploading a friend’s photos to an avatar generator “as a gift”, without her knowledge.
items.rationale: Her likeness travels to someone else’s server without her consent — you ask first, then generate.

points: Generators are a great tool for home creativity — a specific description, iteration, picking the best version.
The wider you publish, the more clearly you label the AI origin and the more carefully you read the license.
Someone else’s face requires consent — always; children’s photos get double protection.
checklist: Could my creation be mistaken for a real photo/recording? If so — is it labeled?
Does the tool’s license allow what I am planning (especially commercially)?
Has every person visible in the material given their consent?
summary.checklistTitle: Checklist — before you publish an AI creation
summary.nextStep: Next step: the module on AI in the family — how to talk with children about all these possibilities and agree on healthy rules.
